title: Quantifiers and Anchors
concept: Quantifiers (+, *, ?) specify how many times a character can repeat; anchors (^, $) lock the pattern to the start or end of the string.

exercises.prompt: What are Regular Expressions (regex) used for in programming?

options.text: Templates used to match and search for patterns in text
A language used to build 3D video games
A tool for compressing images to make them smaller

exercises.explain: Regular expressions (regex) are special text patterns used to search, match, and manipulate text based on specific rules.

cards.title: Control Your Text!
cards.body:
Have you ever wanted to search for words that start *only* with a certain letter, or find passwords that have at least three numbers in a row? 

Normal search tools look anywhere in a word. But with Quantifiers and Anchors, you become the boss of the search! You can decide exactly how many times a character repeats and precisely where it must stand.

cards.title: What are Quantifiers and Anchors?
cards.body:
Quantifiers tell your computer how many times a character can repeat:
• '+' means 1 or more times.
• '*' means 0 or more times.
• '?' means 0 or 1 time (optional).

Anchors lock down where the match can happen:
• '^' locks the match to the very start of the text.
• '$' locks the match to the very end of the text.

cards.title: The VIP Club Bouncers
cards.body:
Imagine a bouncer at a VIP club door. 

If the bouncer is '^' (the Start Anchor), they only let you in if you are the very first person in line. 

If the bouncer is '$' (the End Anchor), they only care about who is standing at the very end of the line. 

Quantifiers (+, *, ?) are like group tickets: '+' means 'bring one or more friends', '*' means 'bring any number of friends or come alone', and '?' means 'bring at most one friend'!

cards.title: Patterns in Action
cards.body:
Let's look at some patterns in action:

• 'cat+' matches 'cat', 'catt', and 'cattt' (at least one 't').
• '^cat' matches 'cat nap' but NOT 'the cat' (must start with 'cat').
• 'cat$' matches 'my cat' but NOT 'cat food' (must end with 'cat').
• '^cat$' matches ONLY the exact string 'cat'.

cards.title: The Star Misconception
cards.body:
Many beginners think that the star '*' quantifier means 'match any character' because of how computer file searches work. 

In Regular Expressions, '*' doesn't mean 'anything'—it means 'zero or more of the character right before it'. So 'a*' matches '', 'a', 'aa', or 'aaa'!

cards.title: Anchored vs. Unanchored
cards.body: See how anchors change what parts of the text are allowed to match. Without anchors, the pattern matches anywhere!

right.text: Only matches the exact single word 'cat' with nothing else.

exercises.prompt: What does the plus '+' quantifier tell the search engine to look for?
exercises.explain: The '+' quantifier stands for 'one or more' of the preceding character. So 'hi+' matches 'hi', 'hii', 'hiii', etc. It requires at least one 'i'.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The pattern 'color?' will match both 'colo' and 'color'.
exercises.explain: The '?' quantifier means 'zero or one time' (optional). Therefore, 'color?' can match 'colo' (zero 'r's) or 'color' (one 'r'). It cannot match 'colorr' because that has two 'r's.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the anchor symbol that forces a match to start only at the very beginning of a string.
exercises.sentence: The [blank] symbol is used to lock a match to the start of a string.
exercises.explain: The '^' anchor forces the match to happen at the very beginning of the string. So '^cat' will match 'catnap' but not 'wildcat'.

exercises.prompt: Which of these strings is a match for the pattern 'cats$'?
exercises.explain: The '$' anchor specifies that the pattern must occur at the end of the text. Option B has 'cats' in the middle, and Option C starts with it but ends with 'sleep'. Only Option A ends with 'cats'.

exercises.prompt: Let's test your master skills! If you write the pattern '^AB+$', which of the following strings will it successfully match?

options.text: Any string that contains 'A' followed by 'B' anywhere inside it.
Only strings that start with 'A', are followed only by one or more 'B's, and then end.
Strings that start with 'B' and end with 'A'.
Strings that contain exactly one 'A' and zero 'B's.

exercises.explain: The anchor '^' ensures the string starts with 'A'. The '+' quantifier requires one or more 'B's right after. The anchor '$' ensures the string ends immediately after those 'B's. Therefore, 'AB' and 'ABBB' match, but 'CAB' (doesn't start with A) and 'ABBC' (doesn't end with B) do not!
